speaker: interviewer
text: --March the 11th, 1996. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the heat was really on by that time. The Arrow Cross were going around looking for Jews. And whoever they caught suspecting to be a Jew, because many Jews were hiding, they took them up to their headquarters. And after furious and-- and the brutal beatings, to be beyond recognition at times, at night, they took them out to this same market, [INAUDIBLE], and they-- they shot them. Now, my commanding officer was arrested. We didn't know what to do here. We are alone. And so I-- I phoned headquarters and I told them that my commanding officer was arrested and they didn't know what to do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After several days, the commanding officer returned and he was terribly, terribly upset. He grew a beard, I remember he looked god awful. And he-- under his breath, he told me, he says, he gave me a feminine name, because I was the-- I was doing all the housework. He gave me a feminine name but he called me under his breath, he says Suzy-- he called me Suzy. He says, take your few things and run and run as far as you can because they are going to deport everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did. I took my things and I started to run. Where do you run? But they said-- You still had the false papers that he had given you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But it-- it didn't mean very much. They said there is a-- a place, a temple that is apparently protected by the Red Cross for Romanian citizens. So somehow I got myself in there. And I was there a few days, a couple of days, two, three days. And then I heard that you can also get some protection papers from the Swiss embassy, that the Swiss embassy is issuing citizenship papers. So I made my way to the Swiss embassy. And although it was-- it was guarded by the Arrow Cross and also by the gendarmes, because the way Budapest is built, it can easily be cut off from either side of the street, because it's more or less built in squares. But I made my way through and I slipped into the Swiss embassy. When I got into the Swiss embassy, there was a Dr. Somogyi there who knew me from before, and I knew him. And I begged him. I says, please take me in, because by that time, there was already an accumulation of people in the Swiss embassy. He says, OK. Go upstairs and try to find yourself a little place, a corner where you can-- you can be. I was there overnight. But the following day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dr. Somogyi came and he gave me one of those Swiss citizenship papers. He says, here is this paper. I don't know how much help it will be, but I'm sorry to tell you that you have to go. You are not allowed to stay here. But what happened, if you ran out from the Swiss embassy, the Arrow Cross and the gendarmes were standing there and they rounded you up right away. They took you away. But I had to get out. I was ordered to get out, so I got out. And lo and behold, they did round us up and they formed a column of certain-- I don't remember how many, about 60, 80 people. And they put us in rows of 5. And I managed to get to the edge of the-- of the-- of my row. And as we were marching, marching, marching-- and they were going around with the-- with their rifles, you know, and their bayonets against us to see that nobody gets out of line.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as we came to one intersection, I managed to tear away and to separate and I started running. So where do I run now? So they said that there is one company, such a brigade that was left in Budapest for the purpose to clean up after the air raids. And they're primary composed of people from my home town. So I got myself in after some lengthy going and walking. I got in there and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got in there. And I asked them that they should take me into the company. They-- they couldn't do it officially. So after a couple of days, in the meantime, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was hiding out in a cellar during the night and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tried to work that I should be taken into this company. So finally after two days, I understand that they bribed one of the officers and they took me in. And as I was ready to go in to the quarters of this compound, I saw three-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three huge trucks came in and gendarmes and Arrow Cross guys were surrounding this company. I took off right away. I didn't go into the quarters. And I start running. But where do you run? And I remember getting onto the street car. And as I got onto the street car, on the platform, as I walked in, I was in front right of two Germans and two Arrow Cross guys, right on the platform. Now, what do I do? If I get off, they'll know there is something wrong. If I go and sit down, they'll detect me that I am Jewish. So I just stood beside them. And I went two stations and I got off. And by that time, I had some inkling that there is an orphanage-- it's called the Columbus Orphanage- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that they are protected by the Red Cross. But to get in there was very difficult, because it was also guarded by the Arrow Cross. By that time, Budapest was bombed very, very heavily and the area was-- a great part of the area was demolished. So I tried to circle around that area and to see where I can find one house where I can go over the fence or under the fence. And luckily, I found one house that had a huge crater. And I slipped into the orphanage there. Now there, I was several days. But I wasn't the only one that was so smart, because people in the city found out about this orphanage and they stared to flock in. Was it an orphanage for children?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was originally meant an orphanage for children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who ran it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was supposedly run by the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: Red Cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this orphanage, I found a cousin-- a daughter of one of my first cousins, who was really an orphan. And she was-- when she saw me, she was flabbergasted. And she says she knows a source that she can give me documents that I shouldn't stay here, because, you know, this is going to be raided. But she was supposed to give me the documents on a Saturday evening. And when I come to see her, she says she's very sorry. She couldn't get the documents. She got me one document, a false document. She couldn't get me any more and she doesn't know when. But I saw what was going on. By that time, hundreds and hundreds of people came in there, try to find refuge. And in the morning, I decided that I have to get out of here. So I was-- with this one document, I was just on my way to the washroom area-- I tried to shave, I shouldn't look like a real refugee or one of the guys from the brigade-- when all of a sudden, again, a whole bunch of trucks came in. Gendarmes and soldiers and Arrow Cross people surrounded the whole outfit, the whole orphanage. And they were shouting, shouting that everybody should line up and line up and line up. And they chased everybody with the rifle butts and everything. And we had to line up. And they started to march us-- there was a small football field where they marched us out and they lined us up there. Hundreds of people by that time were in that orphanage. It was multi-story. It was multi-story building. And they announced, anybody that has a Swedish citizenship paper or Swiss citizenship papers should come forward and show the papers. We thought that this was it. So we ran and we presented the papers. They took the papers and then they told us to go back into the line. So they confiscated the papers and they lined us up. And then they started to sort of segregate people by age. And they lined us up. And again, under heavy, heavy guards with the gendarmes and this Arrow Cross, they marched us to a building. It was called Teleki Square, T-E-L-E-K-I Square. It was a multi-story building and they marched us in there. And this was on a Sunday. On Monday morning, I was-- this time I was very unlucky, because they put me on the third floor and some people were on the second floor. On Monday morning, Wallenberg came with a bus and demanded that all the Swedish-- all the people there with Swedish papers should be released. But people didn't have anymore the Swedish papers. But nonetheless, they broke open by force the portals and they jammed into the bus, so anybody that was on the ground floor got first shot. By the time I came down from the third floor, I was already caught in a trap. I couldn't go out. Some people escaped and Wallenberg took them away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Around 10 o'clock or so, we were ordered to march down and to line up, which we did. We were lined up and we started to be marched toward the railway station. And the railway station-- we arrived at the railway station still under heavy guard. We were lined up in rows and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were broken up in groups of 70 and we marched-- we were put in 70 people per freight car.

speaker: interviewer
text: All men?

speaker: interviewee
text: All men. All men. Young men, middle-aged men. And this procedure was taking on practically most of the day. And towards nightfall, the train moved out. Now, we were traveling on that train, no food, no nothing. But once we were put into the freight cars, there were some young Zionists. How they got there, I don't know, but they were distributing cans of marmalade. That's all they had. So for every freight car, they put in so many cans of marmalade. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were traveling-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday. We were traveling 'til the following Sunday morning, when we reached-- when we reached the Austrian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's seven days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Monday night, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday. Five days 'til we reached the Austrian border. This is usually a distance that actually should take only about a half a day to three quarters of a day, but they kept on sidelining us-- sidelining us because military trains were going back and forth. Then at the Austrian border, we were given a little piece of bread, part of a bread, and some cheese.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the first food you had had.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the first food we got. Luckily, some people had some food acquired you know, there in the orphanage, you know, could acquire some food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were-- we were at the border for maybe a few hours. And then the train took off and we were traveling into Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No water. Everybody was very thirsty. No water. No toilet facilities. People made holes in the-- in the floor. And then there were little windows. People were held. Two or three people would hold people to try to relieve themselves. It was an awful sight. 70 people. We were cramped. You couldn't sleep. When you got up in the morning, you find one leg, one side and 10 people sleeping on it. And this was going on like that. But we didn't get no water. People were very thirsty. And as it happened, the train-- the water came through the wagons, the freight car's roof. People were standing there and fighting for the drops of water. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And 'til-- we traveled 'til about Tuesday evening. Finally, on Tuesday evening, we were already in Germany when the SS-- we were stopped at a railway station and they gave a bucket of water for each wagon. So you can imagine the fight that was going on for that little bit of water. It wasn't 'til the following Thursday morning we woke up. We were in Germany. The train was stopped. And we were-- we were ordered to get out of the freight cars. We were marched out. We were again put into columns. And we were started to be marched for a distance. We saw the sign Bergen-Belsen. And that was our first inclination that we are in a concentration camp. We came into Bergen-Belsen and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they lined us up again in columns. And the SS with their dogs were running up and down with canes beating-- beatings everybody. I was anointed with my first beating there. And it took them time to put us in order. We were-- we were about-- by that time, we were about more than 4,000 people on that train, 4,500 people. We were marched into a tent, a huge tent where there were two SS sitting there on either side with tails. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you had to surrender anything that you had. Now, to backtrack, prior to getting to Bergen-Belsen, the freight car doors opened up and the orders came from the SS, anybody that has any watches, gold pieces should surrender it or else they will be shot on site. So that was the first confiscation. When we arrived in Bergen-Belsen, we were marched to this tent. And you had a little knapsack. You had some things in your pocket, whatever, had to-- you had to surrender before you got-- first you got a beating and then you had to surrender all those things and march through empty-handed. And then we were marched into a barrack. And we were given a wooden bunk, bunk bed, three tiers. And that I remember, it sort of sunk in for the first time that what-- what really took place, what happened. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was like coming out from a dream and I remember breaking out in tears. [DRINKS] Being away such huge distance from your home town, not knowing what happened to your parents, what happened to your sisters. And here you are just nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The following day, we were told to line up and to go to the showers, to leave the clothes on one side and you come out on the other side. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The rumor was that we are going to get some special clothes on the other side. We took the shower, no soap or anything of the sort. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember by the time we came out on the other side, we were told no, no, to go back and to take back our own clothes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we took back our own clothes and we went back to the barracks again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All of a sudden, an order came that everybody should go back to the tent and to pick up their things that they have surrendered. We didn't know what happened, so we went back, we picked up whatever there was, and we were marched back to our barracks. So the rumor was that we will be left with our own civilian clothes and the idea was apparently-- now, these were all rumors-- that we are going to be kept for eventual trade for some trucks or coffee and that type of thing. Nothing was confirmed or officially announced.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were left with our own clothes and, well, that was it. And we were put into those barracks. And from then on, so every morning we would get something that looked like coffee. And around noon time, we would get a turnip soup, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a thick turnip soup. And at night, again, we would get a little bit of that ersatz cafe, you know, coffee substitution. From time-- Were you with any friends? Were you're with people that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: You made friends. You made friends, although I did have some people-- as a matter of fact, I got to know some people in the orphanage that we came together in the-- from the orphanage. But the whole group, that 4,500 people or so, were billeted in several barracks. And they consisted primarily of highly intellectual people, mostly from Budapest, and doctors, lawyers, professors, writers, actors, you name it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were-- we managed to sort of survive on-- every night somebody would give us a little pep talk of some sort. But we were hermetically enclosed. We didn't know what was going on in the outside world at all. We didn't know what happened to anybody. We were in there. And we couldn't-- from time to time, they would take us out to work, either to clean the gutters or to go out in the woods to chop wood for the kitchen. And-- But most of the day you just stayed in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we sort of worked around the camp. We cleaned up. We we weren't-- we weren't in charge of any real heavy physical work. But every morning, the women who were on the other side of the camp, they were mostly in the red barracks, they were marched-- there was a weaving factory adjacent to our camp, which was adjacent to the crematoria. They would be marching through on the main road of the camp. And all of a sudden, somebody yelled-- somebody came and told me that the women would be marched by SS women, surrounded or guarded by SS women with dogs and-- but they would be yelling out names, you know. Wherever you went and you saw a group of people, you would be yelling out names. Maybe you would find somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the boys in my barrack came and told me-- because they would be standing at the gates, you know, that was wired, electrically wired-- that they heard that one girl called my name. So the following day I went out and I yelled out, who called my name? It was mostly Hungarian women-- not mostly, but all of them were Hungarian women. Columns upon columns were going to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sure enough, it was a girl that I knew from my home town, was my girlfriend. And we made a connection and we started to trade a little bit. She would smuggle me in some bread and I would smuggle out-- I would acquire some cigarettes and I would throw it through the fence to her. And she would throw me in some bread. So for the little while, we could-- it made our life a little bit easier. But for Christmas, the Swedish Blue Cross was very good and they sent packages, like little package with some Swedish crackers and little things like-- it wasn't very much. It was a package here was given for 16 people, a package like that. But life was deteriorating, because people were starting to lose their strength. And-- and the worst thing was that every morning at 6 o'clock, we had to be out in the cold for they called it appell, which is a roll call. Now, then the Germans would come and make a head count. And that was one of the worst things, because in northern Germany, it was bitter cold, bitter cold. And we're standing there in that freezing subzero cold from 6 o'clock in the morning. And god forbid if you were found to be-- to be left in the barrack, in the barrack, that you didn't come out, that-- it was a horrible punishment. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He would come at 8 o'clock, at 9 o'clock, at 10 o'clock, at 11 o'clock. And in the meantime, everybody was standing there in freezing cold and you-- you weren't allowed to move. And-- and that-- and during that procedure, many, many people dropped dead, just dropped dead. They just dropped, you know, like flies and you hauled them away. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Famine started to set in very strongly. People became weaker and weaker. And primarily the older people, they-- they couldn't move about. They didn't go-- there was no soap. There was no towels. There was nothing. There was no toilet paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what you did, you know, it was sandy terrain. You went to the cold washbasins there, whatever they had there. So you took a handful of sand and you washed your-- instead of soap, you used sand. And if you went to the-- you had to go to the washroom, you tore out a piece of your-- of your clothing and-- because there was no toilet paper. And you used that and you washed it, you know. And you washed it time and again. You couldn't find a piece of paper if your life depended on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then people were also infected with lice, but infected, you know, where you could be standing with a person and seeing crawling lice-- lice on them, and-- although there was an order that every day you had to stay in the barrack for an hour, an hour and a half-- they called it [INAUDIBLE] to-- to get-- them. but you couldn't. And famine became so very strong that you could be talking to a person [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eyeball-to-eyeball in one minute. And in the next minute, he would just turn his eyes over and drop dead. That's-- that's how weak people became. The first-- the first notion that we got of what was going on was in March of 1944. The American Air Force dropped some leaflets telling us about the Yalta Conference and the proximity of where the front line is standing. But the SS hurriedly went around and they tried to scoop up the leaflets so we wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Break? Yeah.
